text: The surgeon identifies an inflamed appendix with fecolith, adhered to a small loop of the ileum and the lateral pelvic wall. The ileum is stretched using a right hypochondrium grasper, avoiding damage to the serosa. A harmonic device is used with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the spermatic vessel to perform adhesiolysis, separating the intestinal loop from the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully performs adhesiolysis near the peritoneum to prevent thermal spread to the intestinal lobe, while the spermatic vessels are visible below. The appendix is held with the right hypochondrium port, and anterosuperior traction is applied to reveal its base, which has minor adhesions. Using a harmonic device, the surgeon dissects the adhesion bands, keeping the Teflon jaw oriented towards the cecum to avoid transferring vibrations.

The surgeon identifies the appendicular vessel and performs dissection to separate it, ensuring complete access to the base of the appendix. The cecum is slightly mobilized due to previous adhesions with the lateral pelvic wall, requiring further adhesiolysis. Medial traction is applied to improve access, and harmonic dissection is used to cut through the folds.

The surgeon has reached the base of the appendix, which is now free. The genia coli is visible. A Mishra's knot is applied using a grasper to catch the appendix and transfer the loop, with the knot pusher positioned near the cecum. The knot is tightened securely, and the suture is cut with scissors. The harmonic device is used to separate the appendix, with its concavity facing the appendix, ensuring mucosal burning and disinfection of any fecal matter.

The surgeon carefully separates the appendix from its base using a harmonic device, ensuring the teflon jaw does not touch the suture or cecum to prevent damage. The appendix is successfully detached, and an endo catch bag is used to contain the appendix, which is then pushed through a ten millimeter port to prevent contamination of the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo catch bag, which has a self-retaining metal ring, and carefully drops the appendix inside. The grasper is used to push the appendix further into the bag, ensuring it does not fall out. The persisting suture is pulled to close the bag, which is then extracted through the ten millimeter port. An artery forceps may be used to assist in stretching the wound slightly for easier removal of the endo bag. The port is closed using number one Vicryl suture with suture passers to prevent hernia formation due to the stretched wound.

The surgeon applies betadine to the base of the appendix for disinfection, ensuring excess betadine is suctioned out to prevent residue. The appendix is visibly tightened, confirming the cessation of gas leakage.
